query: react pure functions of props and state interview question

selection.greatFrontendReasonIfOmitted: No exact-topic GreatFrontEnd page was confirmed for this React prompt, so broader React question-bank pages would be weaker competitors than topic-direct sources.

competitors.label: Components and Hooks must be pure
competitors.selectionReason: This is the canonical React rule page for purity, idempotent rendering, and side-effect-free component bodies.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative explanation of idempotent rendering and why React needs pure component logic.
theirGaps: It is more rule-centric and less compressed around the production consequences of impurity in StrictMode and concurrent rendering.

realWorldUseCases: That purity lets React freely re-run renders, pause/abort work, and diff trees safely without causing “real-world” side effects.
followUpConfusion: If running the component body twice would cause anything “real” to happen twice

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain purity accurately, but our page is more explicit about the real production failures: duplicate network calls, analytics, mutations, and render-time races.
actionableExamples: The canonical page still includes strong examples of idempotent and non-idempotent code, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up that side effects belong in effects and event handlers because React may replay render work.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny render-time fetch versus effect-based fetch example later if we want more literal code than the current rule-of-thumb block.

competitors.winReason: The official docs are authoritative, but our page is stronger for interview delivery because it ties purity directly to replayable rendering, StrictMode, and concrete side-effect failures.

competitors.label: Pure Render
competitors.selectionReason: React Style Guide has a topic-direct rule page on pure render logic, side-effect isolation, and StrictMode/concurrent implications.
theirStrengths: It is practical on common render-time mistakes like fetches, mutations, and duplicate work in StrictMode.
theirGaps: It is longer and less compact around the exact interview framing of UI as a pure computation of props, state, and context.

realWorldUseCases: Pure rendering improves predictability and testing, but side effects must live in effects/hooks.
followUpConfusion: Render phase should only compute the next UI tree.

realWorldUseCases: The style guide is practical, but our page is more explicit about why purity unlocks pause, abort, restart, and safe reconciliation behavior.
actionableExamples: Its broken-versus-fixed examples are solid, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up that purity is about render determinism, not about never using effects.

actionableExamples: Add one localStorage-in-render example later if we want another concrete impurity case next to fetch and subscription examples.

competitors.winReason: This tutorial is practical, but our page is stronger because it frames purity as the contract that lets React replay render work safely, not just as a style rule.

competitors.label: Must React components act like pure functions with respect to their props?
competitors.selectionReason: This community discussion is a direct practical reference for the purity expectation around component inputs and rendering semantics.
theirStrengths: It addresses the exact purity question in plain language.
theirGaps: It is narrower and less complete on render versus commit, side-effect placement, and restartable rendering.

realWorldUseCases: Connect it to render vs commit, Concurrent Rendering, StrictMode double-invocation, memoization, and predictable reconciliation.
followUpConfusion: Side effects belong in event handlers (user actions) or effects

realWorldUseCases: The thread explains the idea, but our page is stronger on the concrete React runtime features that depend on purity.
actionableExamples: Community explanations can still be concrete, so the example criterion stays tied.
followUpConfusion: Our answer is clearer on the likely follow-up that a component can read props, state, and context during render without losing purity as long as the computation stays deterministic.

actionableExamples: Keep one explicit 'render twice' example visible so the duplicate-side-effect failure mode stays concrete.

competitors.winReason: The community thread defines the idea, but our page is stronger because it connects purity to the exact runtime guarantees React relies on.
